eli3: A vector is a list of numbers that points in some direction — like an arrow from the origin to a point in space. [3, 4] points 3 units right and 4 units up. A matrix is a grid of numbers, like a spreadsheet. It can stretch, rotate, or flip vectors.
Think of a matrix as a machine: you feed it a vector, and it spits out a new vector. Each row of the matrix tells you how much of each input number to mix into each output number.
Vectors represent things like position, velocity, or word embeddings. Matrices represent transformations, datasets (rows = examples, columns = features), or systems of equations.

whenWhy.title: Representing data in ML
whenWhy.body: Each row of a data matrix X ∈ ℝⁿˣᵖ is one example with p features. Linear regression solves Xβ ≈ y. Neural networks are compositions of matrix multiplications with nonlinearities.

whenWhy.title: Working with embeddings
whenWhy.body: Word2vec and BERT produce dense vectors. Similarity between documents is cosine of their vector angle. Matrix factorization (SVD, NMF) decomposes term-document matrices for topic modeling.

whenWhy.title: Transformations and coordinate systems
whenWhy.body: PCA finds a new coordinate system (rotation) where axes are uncorrelated. CNNs use convolution matrices to detect local patterns. Computer graphics uses 4×4 matrices for 3D transformations.

whenWhy.title: Solving systems of linear equations
whenWhy.body: Ax = b represents a system. Gaussian elimination, LU decomposition, or matrix inversion solve it. Used in optimization (Newton's method), circuit analysis, and structural engineering.

deepDiveIntro: A vector v ∈ ℝⁿ is an element of n-dimensional real vector space. It has magnitude (norm) ||v|| and direction. A matrix A ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ represents a linear transformation T: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ via matrix-vector multiplication: (Av)ᵢ = Σⱼ Aᵢⱼvⱼ. Linearity means T(av + bw) = aT(v) + bT(w).

Matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of linear transformations. If A: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ and B: ℝᵐ → ℝᵖ, then BA: ℝⁿ → ℝᵖ. The identity matrix I has Iᵢⱼ = δᵢⱼ (Kronecker delta). The inverse A⁻¹ exists iff det(A) ≠ 0, giving the unique solution to Ax = b as x = A⁻¹b.

deepDive.heading: Vector Spaces
deepDive.body: A vector space over a field F (usually ℝ) is a set V with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying: closure, associativity, commutativity, identity (0 vector), inverses, and distributivity. Basis {v₁,…,vₙ} spans V linearly independently. Every v ∈ V has unique coordinates in a basis. Dimension = size of any basis.

deepDive.heading: Matrix Multiplication
deepDive.body: For A ∈ ℝᵐˣⁿ and B ∈ ℝⁿˣᵖ, the product AB ∈ ℝᵐˣᵖ has (AB)ᵢₖ = Σⱼ AᵢⱼBⱼₖ. Not commutative: AB ≠ BA in general. Associative: (AB)C = A(BC). Transpose: (AB)ᵀ = BᵀAᵀ. Computational complexity: O(mnp) for naive multiplication.

deepDive.heading: Rank and Null Space
deepDive.body: The column space C(A) = span of columns, dimension = rank(A). The null space N(A) = {x: Ax = 0}. Rank-nullity theorem: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n (number of columns). Full rank means rank = min(m, n). For square matrices, full rank ↔ invertible.

deepDive.heading: Special Matrix Types
deepDive.body: Symmetric: A = Aᵀ. Positive definite: xᵀAx > 0 for all x ≠ 0. Orthogonal: QᵀQ = I (preserves lengths and angles). Diagonal: nonzeros only on diagonal. Triangular: all zeros above (lower) or below (upper) diagonal. Sparse: mostly zeros — stored efficiently for large systems.

deepDive.heading: Geometric Interpretation
deepDive.body: Matrices encode linear transformations: scaling (diagonal), rotation (orthogonal with det=1), reflection (orthogonal with det=-1), shearing, projection (idempotent: P² = P). Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) reveals that every matrix is a rotation-scale-rotation composition.

takeaways: Vectors are lists with magnitude/direction; matrices are linear transformation machines.
Rank = dimension of column space; nullity = dimension of null space; sum to n.
A is invertible ⟺ full rank ⟺ det(A) ≠ 0 ⟺ columns are linearly independent.
Special types: symmetric, orthogonal, diagonal, positive definite — each with useful properties.

edgeCases.q: When does Ax = b have no solution?
edgeCases.a: When b is not in the column space of A (system is inconsistent). This happens when rank([A|b]) > rank(A). The least squares solution x̂ = (AᵀA)⁻¹Aᵀb minimizes ||Ax - b||².
AB applies B then A; BA applies A then B. Different order = different result. Example: A = rotation 90°, B = scaling [2,1]. AB ≠ BA. This is why matrix exponentials e^(A+B) ≠ e^A e^B unless AB = BA.

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between row rank and column rank?
edgeCases.a: They're always equal (rank theorem). But column rank = dimension of output space, row rank = dimension of input space that maps to nonzero outputs. For non-square matrices, these are different spaces but have the same dimension.

sources.label: Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra (6th ed.)
